```{r abundance-scatter, fig.width=11, fig.height=8}
    same_platform = identical(sort(p), c("afekta", "cembio"))
  )
}))

ggplot(abund_pairs, aes(x = x, y = y, colour = same_platform)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0,
              colour = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE,
              colour = "black", linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(`FALSE` = "grey40",
                                 `TRUE`  = "#E64B35"),
                      labels = c("Cross-platform", "Same-platform"),
                      name = NULL) +
  facet_grid(polarity ~ pair, scales = "free") +
  labs(title    = "Inter-lab relative abundance (log2 AUC / lab median)",
       subtitle = "Consensus peaks present in all 4 labs",
       x = "Lab 1 (log2 AUC / lab median)",
       y = "Lab 2 (log2 AUC / lab median)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "top")
```

The same-platform Agilent–Agilent pair (afekta:cembio) sits at the top
of every polarity's Pearson r table — the ceiling for what
intensity-based agreement looks like in this dataset. Cross-platform
pairs trail it, and the slope of the OLS fits (deviating from the red
y=x identity line) shows where the residual lab-pair-specific scaling
remains *after* normalisation to the lab median.

## 3.5 Cross-Lab Pearson on Scalar Metrics

Per consensus peak we compare four scalar metrics from MsQuality across
labs: intensity-scale metrics `areaUnderTic` and `peakProminence`
(log2-transformed before correlating, since they span orders of
magnitude) and shape metrics `xicFwhm` (FWHM in seconds) and
`gaussianSimilarity.gaussian_similarity` (0–1, how Gaussian the peak
shape is). For every pair of labs and every polarity, we compute Pearson
r over peaks present in all four labs. Cells with fewer than 50
contributing peaks are dropped to keep the estimates stable.

```{r cross-lab-pearson-table}
metric_cols  <- c("areaUnderTic", "peakProminence",
                  "xicFwhm", "gaussianSimilarity.gaussian_similarity")
log2_metrics <- c("areaUnderTic", "peakProminence")
metric_type  <- c(areaUnderTic = "Intensity", peakProminence = "Intensity",
                  xicFwhm = "Shape",
                  `gaussianSimilarity.gaussian_similarity` = "Shape")
lab_pairs    <- combn(c("afekta", "cembio", "hmgu", "icl"), 2, simplify = FALSE)

compute_metric_corr <- function(data, pol_label) {
  pk_complete <- data |>
    group_by(consensus_id) |> filter(n() == 4) |> ungroup()

  map_dfr(metric_cols, function(m) {
    wide <- pk_complete |>
      select(consensus_id, lab, all_of(m)) |>
      pivot_wider(names_from = lab, values_from = all_of(m))

    map_dfr(lab_pairs, function(p) {
      if (!all(p %in% names(wide))) return(NULL)
      vals <- wide |> select(all_of(p)) |> drop_na()
      if (nrow(vals) < 50) return(NULL)            # robustness: n >= 50
      if (m %in% log2_metrics)
        vals <- vals |> mutate(across(everything(), \(x) log2(pmax(x, 1e-9))))
      tibble(
        polarity      = pol_label,
        metric_type   = unname(metric_type[m]),
        metric        = m,
        pair          = paste(p, collapse = ":"),
        same_platform = identical(sort(p), c("afekta", "cembio")),
        r             = cor(vals[[1]], vals[[2]], use = "complete.obs"),
        n             = nrow(vals)
      )
    })
  })
}

metric_corr <- bind_rows(
  compute_metric_corr(pk_metrics |> filter(polarity_label == "Positive"), "Positive"),
  compute_metric_corr(pk_metrics |> filter(polarity_label == "Negative"), "Negative")
)
stopifnot(nrow(metric_corr) > 0)

# Unified table: rows = (polarity, metric_type, metric), columns = lab pair
metric_corr |>
  mutate(r = round(r, 3),
         metric_type = factor(metric_type, levels = c("Intensity", "Shape"))) |>
  select(polarity, metric_type, metric, pair, r) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = pair, values_from = r) |>
  arrange(polarity, metric_type, metric) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Cross-lab Pearson r per metric and lab pair, by polarity. Same-platform pair = afekta:cembio. Cells with n < 50 dropped.")
```

```{r cross-lab-pearson-summary}
# Same-platform vs cross-platform median r per (polarity, metric)
metric_corr |>
  group_by(polarity, metric_type, metric, same_platform) |>
  summarise(median_r = median(r, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = same_platform, values_from = median_r,
              names_prefix = "sp_") |>
  rename(same_platform = sp_TRUE, cross_platform = sp_FALSE) |>
  mutate(gap            = round(same_platform  - cross_platform, 3),
         same_platform  = round(same_platform,  3),
         cross_platform = round(cross_platform, 3),
         metric_type    = factor(metric_type, levels = c("Intensity", "Shape"))) |>
  arrange(polarity, metric_type, metric) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Same-platform vs cross-platform median Pearson r per metric. Gap = same-platform − cross-platform.")
```

```{r cross-lab-pearson-plot}
#| fig-cap: "**Figure 3.3**: Cross-lab Pearson r per metric and lab pair, split by polarity. Intensity metrics on the left, shape metrics on the right; same-platform pair (afekta:cembio) marked in red."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 7

ggplot(metric_corr,
       aes(x = metric, y = r, color = same_platform, group = pair)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, position = position_dodge(width = 0.5)) +
  geom_text(aes(label = pair),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.5),
            size = 2.3, hjust = -0.15) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(`FALSE` = "grey50", `TRUE` = "#E64B35"),
                     labels = c("Cross-platform", "Same-platform"),
                     name = NULL) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey70") +
  facet_grid(polarity ~ factor(metric_type, levels = c("Intensity", "Shape")),
             scales = "free_x", space = "free_x") +
  labs(title = "Cross-Lab Pearson Correlation per Metric",
       x = NULL, y = "Cross-Lab Pearson r") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))
```

## 3.6 Inter-Lab Retention Time Drift

How much does the RT of the same compound differ across labs? Per
consensus peak detected in all four labs we have 4 apex RTs and 6
pairwise Δ_RT values; we report the distribution of |Δ_RT| per lab
pair, by polarity, and check whether drift is constant across the
gradient or RT-dependent (LOESS smooth).

```{r rt-drift-pairwise}
# One row per (consensus_id, lab pair): consensus RT (mean across 4 labs)
# and signed Δ_RT (lab2 − lab1).
apex_wide <- pk_metrics |>
  filter(!is.na(apex_rt)) |>
  select(consensus_id, lab, polarity_label, apex_rt) |>
  group_by(consensus_id) |>
  filter(n() == 4) |>
  ungroup() |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = lab, values_from = apex_rt) |>
  drop_na(any_of(c("afekta", "cembio", "hmgu", "icl"))) |>
  mutate(consensus_rt = (afekta + cembio + hmgu + icl) / 4)

delta_rt <- map_dfr(lab_pairs, function(p) {
  apex_wide |>
    transmute(
      consensus_id,
      polarity      = polarity_label,
      consensus_rt,
      pair          = paste(p, collapse = ":"),
      delta_rt      = .data[[p[2]]] - .data[[p[1]]],
      abs_delta_rt  = abs(delta_rt),
      same_platform = identical(sort(p), c("afekta", "cembio"))
    )
})
```

```{r rt-drift-table}
delta_rt |>
  group_by(polarity, pair, same_platform) |>
  summarise(
    n                = n(),
    median_abs_drift = round(median(abs_delta_rt), 3),
    pct95_abs_drift  = round(quantile(abs_delta_rt, 0.95), 3),
    max_abs_drift    = round(max(abs_delta_rt), 3),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(polarity, desc(median_abs_drift)) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Inter-lab RT drift |Δ_RT| per lab pair and polarity (seconds).")
```

```{r rt-drift-plot}
#| fig-cap: "**Figure 3.4**: Inter-lab Δ_RT vs consensus RT, faceted by lab pair (rows = polarity, columns = lab pair). Same-platform pair (afekta:cembio) marked in red. Black LOESS smooth highlights whether drift is constant across the gradient or RT-dependent."
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 7

ggplot(delta_rt,
       aes(x = consensus_rt, y = delta_rt, color = same_platform)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey60") +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.7,
              color = "black") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(`FALSE` = "grey50", `TRUE` = "#E64B35"),
                     labels = c("Cross-platform", "Same-platform"),
                     name = NULL) +
  facet_grid(polarity ~ pair, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(title    = "Inter-lab RT drift across the gradient",
       subtitle = "Δ_RT (lab2 − lab1) vs consensus RT, LOESS smooth in black",
       x = "Consensus RT (s)", y = "Δ_RT (s)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        strip.text.y = element_text(size = 9))
```

The same-platform pair (afekta:cembio) sets the floor for what RT
agreement is achievable in this trial; cross-platform pairs scatter
more widely and any RT-dependent trend in the LOESS line indicates
gradient-dependent drift that no scalar matching tolerance would catch.

## 3.7 Synthesis

Part 3 partitions the residual cross-lab variance after feature
extraction (Part 1) and intensity normalisation (Part 2) into three
axes: intensity, retention time, and peak shape.

**Per-lab quality (§3.3)** establishes the baseline: absolute intensity
distributions shift between labs while width-like shape descriptors
overlap more, locating most of the inter-lab variance on the intensity
axis from the outset.

**Intensity-scale agreement (§3.4, §3.5)** is concentrated and
structured. Inter-lab relative-abundance correlation places the
same-platform Agilent–Agilent pair (afekta:cembio) at the top of both
polarities — the practical ceiling for intensity reproducibility.
Cross-lab Pearson on `areaUnderTic` and `peakProminence` confirms this
gap (same-platform r is +0.08–0.29 above cross-platform median). This
is the variance Part 2's RT-dependent normalisation addresses and the
variance Part 1's ceiling analysis bounds — partially recoverable
through computational normalisation.

**Retention time (§3.6)** drifts substantially across labs.
Same-platform |Δ_RT| acts as the achievable floor; cross-platform pairs
show larger median drift and gradient-dependent trends. Absolute RT
cannot be aligned across labs in this dataset, and a fixed-tolerance
RT match would mis-rank many cross-platform peaks — any cross-lab
correspondence step needs apex-aligned (not absolute-RT) matching.

**Shape metrics** (`xicFwhm`, `gaussianSimilarity`) show no platform
structure: same-platform Pearson r is within 0.04 of cross-platform
median for every shape metric in every polarity. Treating apex-aligned
EIC-profile Pearson r as a binary classifier of matched vs
RT-gap-controlled random pairs gives AUROC ≈ 0.5 across all six lab
pairs in both polarities — the full profile carries no cross-lab
discriminative signal beyond what the scalar shape metrics already
capture.

The implication for multi-centre metabolomics is methodological, not
biological. Intensity-scale variance is concentrated, structured, and
partially fixable by normalisation. Shape-descriptor variance is
diffuse, unstructured by platform, and not addressable by
post-acquisition processing of the EIC. Cross-lab QC workflows should
use intensity-aggregated metrics (peak area, prominence, height) for
reproducibility assessment; ratio-based shape descriptors (asymmetry,
tailing, FWHM) carry no cross-lab signal in this regime and should not
be used as cross-lab QC metrics. Whether the residual shape variance
reflects acquisition-setting differences (which a more strictly
standardised protocol could address) or instrument-hardware differences
(which it could not) is not separable in our study design.

---

# Part 4: The Hardware Limitation - Defining the Reproducibility Ceiling {#hardware}

**Core claim:** Instrument chemistry imposes an irreducible variance ceiling
even after optimal processing. Same-platform ICC defines the upper bound.

## 4.1 Platform Effects on Peak Intensity Correlations

### 4.1.1 Cross-Lab Correlation: Peak Area

Pairwise cross-lab Pearson correlations on consensus peaks.

```{r area-correlation-matrix}
#| fig-cap: "**Figure 4.1**: Cross-lab correlation matrix for Peak Area (log2-transformed). Same-platform correlations are high (~0.81), while cross-platform pairs are moderate (0.43–0.54)."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 7

library(GGally)

# Pivot to wide format: rows = consensus_id, columns = lab
area_wide <- pk_metrics |>
  filter(!is.na(areaUnderTic), areaUnderTic > 0) |>
  group_by(consensus_id) |>
  filter(n() == 4) |>  # Keep only peaks detected in all 4 labs
  ungroup() |>
  select(consensus_id, lab, areaUnderTic) |>
  mutate(log2_area = log2(areaUnderTic)) |>
  select(-areaUnderTic) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = lab, values_from = log2_area)

# Create correlation matrix plot
ggpairs(
  area_wide |> select(-consensus_id),
  title = "Cross-Lab Correlation Matrix: Peak Area (log2)",
  upper = list(continuous = wrap("cor", size = 5, color = "blue")),
  lower = list(continuous = wrap("points", alpha = 0.3, size = 0.5)),
  diag = list(continuous = wrap("densityDiag", alpha = 0.5))
) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**Findings**:
- Agilent-Agilent (afekta-cembio): r = 0.81
- Cross-platform (Agilent vs Waters/Sciex): r = 0.43–0.54
- Waters-Sciex (hmgu-icl): r = 0.43

Same-platform r is ~50% higher than cross-platform; all r > 0.4.

## 4.2 The Ceiling: Same-Platform vs Cross-Platform ICC Across RT Deciles

Same-platform ICC (Agilent-Agilent) vs cross-platform ICC (all 4 labs), by
RT decile. The gap defines the hardware ceiling.

```{r hardware-ceiling-analysis}
#| fig-cap: "**Figure 4.2**: ICC by RT Decile for Same-Platform (Agilent-Agilent) vs Cross-Platform (all 4 labs). Same-platform labs maintain high ICC (0.80–0.95) across all deciles, while cross-platform ICC drops in late deciles (D8–D10), revealing where instrument chemistry dominates."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 7

# Ensure pk_norm is available with rt_decile and area_loess columns (from Part 2)
if (!exists("pk_norm") || !("rt_decile" %in% names(pk_norm))) {
  stop("pk_norm with rt_decile column must be loaded from Part 2")
}

# Function to compute ICC by RT decile
compute_icc_by_decile <- function(pk_data, lab_filter = NULL, label = "") {
  if (!is.null(lab_filter)) {
    pk_data <- pk_data |> filter(lab %in% lab_filter)
  }

  results <- pk_data |>
    filter(!is.na(rt_decile), !is.na(area_loess)) |>
    group_by(consensus_id, rt_decile) |>
    filter(n() == length(lab_filter %||% unique(pk_data$lab))) |>
    ungroup() |>
    group_by(rt_decile) |>
    summarise(
      n_peaks = n_distinct(consensus_id),
      icc = tryCatch({
        wide <- current_data() |>
          select(lab, area_loess) |>
          pivot_wider(names_from = lab, values_from = area_loess)

        # Compute ICC(2,k) for this decile
        k <- ncol(wide)
        if (k < 2 || nrow(wide) < 2) {
          NA_real_
        } else {
          wide_mat <- as.matrix(wide)
          grand_mean <- mean(wide_mat, na.rm = TRUE)
          target_means <- rowMeans(wide_mat, na.rm = TRUE)
          ms_targets <- sum((target_means - grand_mean)^2, na.rm = TRUE) / (nrow(wide) - 1)
          residuals <- wide_mat - target_means
          ms_error <- sum(residuals^2, na.rm = TRUE) / ((nrow(wide) - 1) * (k - 1))
          (ms_targets - ms_error) / (ms_targets + (k - 1) * ms_error)
        }
      }, error = function(e) NA_real_),
      .groups = "drop"
    )

  if (!is.null(lab_filter)) {
    results <- results |> mutate(Configuration = label)
  }
  return(results)
}

# Compute ICC by decile for Same-Platform (Agilent only: afekta + cembio)
same_platform_by_decile <- pk_norm |>
  filter(lab %in% c("afekta", "cembio")) |>
  filter(!is.na(rt_decile), !is.na(area_loess)) |>
  group_by(consensus_id, rt_decile) |>
  filter(n() == 2) |>  # Only peaks in both Agilent labs
  ungroup() |>
  group_by(rt_decile) |>
  summarise(
    n_peaks = n_distinct(consensus_id),
    icc = tryCatch({
      wide <- current_data() |>
        select(lab, area_loess) |>
        pivot_wider(names_from = lab, values_from = area_loess)

      if (nrow(wide) < 2) {
        NA_real_
      } else {
        wide_mat <- as.matrix(wide)
        grand_mean <- mean(wide_mat, na.rm = TRUE)
        target_means <- rowMeans(wide_mat, na.rm = TRUE)
        ms_targets <- sum((target_means - grand_mean)^2, na.rm = TRUE) / (nrow(wide) - 1)
        residuals <- wide_mat - target_means
        ms_error <- sum(residuals^2, na.rm = TRUE) / (nrow(wide) - 1)
        (ms_targets - ms_error) / (ms_targets + ms_error)
      }
    }, error = function(e) NA_real_),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(Configuration = "Same-Platform\n(Agilent-Agilent)")

# Compute ICC by decile for Cross-Platform (all 4 labs)
cross_platform_by_decile <- pk_norm |>
  filter(!is.na(rt_decile), !is.na(area_loess)) |>
  group_by(consensus_id, rt_decile) |>
  filter(n() == 4) |>  # Only peaks detected in all 4 labs
  ungroup() |>
  group_by(rt_decile) |>
  summarise(
    n_peaks = n_distinct(consensus_id),
    icc = tryCatch({
      wide <- current_data() |>
        select(lab, area_loess) |>
        pivot_wider(names_from = lab, values_from = area_loess)

      if (nrow(wide) < 2) {
        NA_real_
      } else {
        wide_mat <- as.matrix(wide)
        grand_mean <- mean(wide_mat, na.rm = TRUE)
        target_means <- rowMeans(wide_mat, na.rm = TRUE)
        ms_targets <- sum((target_means - grand_mean)^2, na.rm = TRUE) / (nrow(wide) - 1)
        residuals <- wide_mat - target_means
        ms_error <- sum(residuals^2, na.rm = TRUE) / ((nrow(wide) - 1) * 3)
        (ms_targets - ms_error) / (ms_targets + 3 * ms_error)
      }
    }, error = function(e) NA_real_),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(Configuration = "Cross-Platform\n(4 labs, 4 vendors)")

# Combine results
ceiling_by_decile <- bind_rows(
  same_platform_by_decile,
  cross_platform_by_decile
) |>
  mutate(
    Configuration = factor(Configuration,
                          levels = c("Same-Platform\n(Agilent-Agilent)",
                                    "Cross-Platform\n(4 labs, 4 vendors)"))
  )

ggplot(ceiling_by_decile, aes(x = rt_decile, y = icc, color = Configuration,
                               linetype = Configuration, size = Configuration)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Same-Platform\n(Agilent-Agilent)" = "#4DBBD5",
                                 "Cross-Platform\n(4 labs, 4 vendors)" = "#E64B35")) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("Same-Platform\n(Agilent-Agilent)" = "solid",
                                    "Cross-Platform\n(4 labs, 4 vendors)" = "dashed")) +
  scale_size_manual(values = c("Same-Platform\n(Agilent-Agilent)" = 1.0,
                               "Cross-Platform\n(4 labs, 4 vendors)" = 0.8)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dotted", color = "gray50", alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1.0), breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)) +
  labs(
    title = "The Hardware Ceiling Across RT Deciles",
    subtitle = "Same-Platform ICC remains high (0.80–0.95) across deciles; Cross-Platform ICC drops in late deciles (instrument chemistry effect)",
    x = "RT Decile", y = "ICC", color = "Configuration", linetype = "Configuration"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "gray90", linewidth = 0.3),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0))
```

---

# Part 5: Annotation Ceiling Across Labs {#annotation-ceiling}

Parts 1–4 quantify cross-lab variance on detected peaks. Part 5 asks
the downstream question: after every mitigation step (peak picking,
normalisation, consensus matching) what does the *annotation outcome*
look like across labs? We use the manually curated SIRIUS annotations
(`3_annotation_manual/HE/3_Sirius_curation/`) where each lab's
annotation table assigns one or more `(compound, adduct)` labels to
chrom_peak_ids.

The ceiling is operationalised three ways:

1. **RT and m/z spread per annotated compound** (§5.2, §5.3) — how
   much do labs disagree on *where* the same compound elutes / *what
   m/z* they observe.
2. **Per-lab coverage and confidence tiers** (§5.4) — how many
   compounds each lab annotates and how often the labs converge on the
   same consensus peak.
3. **SIRIUS structure rank vs RT shift** (§5.5) — does the residual
   RT variance from Part 3 degrade structural identification?

## 5.1 Load Curated Annotations

```{r p5-setup, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
labs        <- c("afekta", "cembio", "hmgu", "icl")
curated_dir <- here::here("3_annotation_manual", "HE", "3_Sirius_curation")
std_path    <- here::here("1_preprocessing", "standards.xlsx")

read_lab <- function(lab) {
  f <- file.path(curated_dir,
                 paste0(lab, "_lab_annotations_sirius_curated.xlsx"))
  x <- as.data.frame(read_xlsx(f), check.names = FALSE)
  x$lab <- lab
  x
}

raw <- lapply(labs, read_lab)
names(raw) <- labs

unnest_lab <- function(x) {
  cps <- strsplit(as.character(x$chrom_peak_id), "|", fixed = TRUE)
  ads <- strsplit(as.character(x$adduct),        "|", fixed = TRUE)
  n   <- lengths(cps)
  stopifnot(all(n == lengths(ads)))

  idx <- rep(seq_len(nrow(x)), n)
  out <- data.frame(
    lab           = x$lab[idx],
    compound      = trimws(x$target_ChEBI.name[idx]),
    chrom_peak_id = trimws(unlist(cps, use.names = FALSE)),
    adduct        = trimws(unlist(ads, use.names = FALSE)),
    rtmed         = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(x$rtmed[idx])),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  out$mz <- NA_real_
  for (a in unique(out$adduct)) {
    col <- paste0("mz_", a)
    if (col %in% colnames(x)) {
      sel     <- out$adduct == a
      row_idx <- idx[sel]
      out$mz[sel] <- suppressWarnings(as.numeric(x[[col]][row_idx]))
    }
  }
  out
}

long <- do.call(rbind, lapply(raw, unnest_lab))
long <- long[!is.na(long$compound) & long$compound != "", ]
rownames(long) <- NULL

# Per-(compound, lab) SIRIUS structure rank (1 = top hit).
rank_lab <- do.call(rbind, lapply(raw, function(x) {
  data.frame(
    lab            = x$lab,
    compound       = trimws(x$target_ChEBI.name),
    structure_rank = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(x$best_structurePerIdRank)),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}))
rank_lab <- rank_lab[!is.na(rank_lab$compound) & rank_lab$compound != "", ]
rownames(rank_lab) <- NULL

cat("Curated annotation rows (long):", nrow(long), "\n")
cat("Rank rows:", nrow(rank_lab), "\n")
```

## 5.2 RT Deviation Between Labs

Per compound, the lab-level `rtmed` (one value per lab) is summarised
into a cross-lab sd and range. Compounds detected by only one lab are
dropped.

```{r p5-rt-deviation}
rt_lab <- aggregate(rtmed ~ compound + lab, data = long,
                    FUN = function(v) mean(v, na.rm = TRUE))

rt_stats <- aggregate(rtmed ~ compound, data = rt_lab,
                      FUN = function(v) c(n     = length(v),
                                          mean  = mean(v, na.rm = TRUE),
                                          sd    = sd(v,   na.rm = TRUE),
                                          range = diff(range(v, na.rm = TRUE))))
rt_stats <- do.call(data.frame, rt_stats)
colnames(rt_stats) <- c("compound", "n_labs", "rt_mean", "rt_sd", "rt_range")
rt_stats <- rt_stats[order(-rt_stats$rt_sd), ]
knitr::kable(head(rt_stats, 10),
             caption = "Top 10 compounds by between-lab RT sd (seconds).",
             digits = 2)
```

```{r p5-rt-deviation-plot, fig.width=7, fig.height=4.5}
#| fig-cap: "**Figure 5.1**: Between-lab RT sd per compound vs number of labs detecting it. Compounds annotated by more labs do not necessarily converge in RT — the spread is set by the cross-platform RT drift quantified in Section 3.6."
ggplot(rt_stats[rt_stats$n_labs > 1, ],
       aes(x = n_labs, y = rt_sd)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(x = "Number of labs detecting compound",
       y = "RT sd across labs (s)",
       title = "Between-lab RT variability per compound") +
  theme_bw()
```

```{r p5-rt-top-plot, fig.width=8, fig.height=5}
#| fig-cap: "**Figure 5.2**: Per-lab RT for the 10 compounds with the highest cross-lab RT spread. Visualises the absolute drift between labs at the annotation level."
worst <- head(rt_stats[rt_stats$n_labs > 1, ], 10)
sub <- rt_lab[rt_lab$compound %in% worst$compound, ]
sub$compound <- factor(sub$compound, levels = worst$compound)

ggplot(sub, aes(x = compound, y = rtmed, colour = lab)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "RT (s)",
       title = "Top 10 compounds by RT spread") +
  theme_bw()
```

The RT spread at annotated compounds is the practical consequence of
the cross-lab |Δ_RT| distribution from Section 3.6. Compounds where
the spread exceeds the consensus-grouping tolerance (15 s) are
candidates for being split into separate consensus peaks downstream —
which feeds directly into the confidence tiers in §5.4.

## 5.3 m/z Deviation Between Labs

Per `(compound, adduct)`, spread across labs of observed m/z,
converted to ppm against the across-lab mean.

```{r p5-mz-deviation}
mz_lab <- aggregate(mz ~ compound + adduct + lab, data = long,
                    FUN = function(v) mean(v, na.rm = TRUE))
mz_lab <- mz_lab[!is.na(mz_lab$mz), ]

mz_stats <- aggregate(mz ~ compound + adduct, data = mz_lab,
                      FUN = function(v) c(n     = length(v),
                                          mean  = mean(v, na.rm = TRUE),
                                          sd    = sd(v,   na.rm = TRUE),
                                          range = diff(range(v, na.rm = TRUE))))
mz_stats <- do.call(data.frame, mz_stats)
colnames(mz_stats) <- c("compound", "adduct", "n_labs",
                        "mz_mean", "mz_sd", "mz_range")
mz_stats$ppm <- 1e6 * mz_stats$mz_range / mz_stats$mz_mean
mz_stats <- mz_stats[order(-mz_stats$ppm), ]
knitr::kable(head(mz_stats[, c("compound", "adduct", "n_labs",
                               "mz_mean", "mz_range", "ppm")], 10),
             caption = "Top 10 (compound, adduct) by between-lab m/z spread (ppm).",
             digits = 3)
```

```{r p5-mz-deviation-plot, fig.width=7, fig.height=4.5}
#| fig-cap: "**Figure 5.3**: Between-lab m/z spread per (compound, adduct), in ppm. Adducts with consistently wide spreads point at lab-specific calibration drift rather than chemistry."
ggplot(mz_stats[mz_stats$n_labs > 1, ],
       aes(x = adduct, y = ppm)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(x = "Adduct", y = "m/z range across labs (ppm)",
       title = "Between-lab m/z variability per (compound, adduct)") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

m/z agreement is tighter than RT agreement — most ppm values sit well
below the 20-ppm consensus-grouping tolerance, so m/z is rarely the
limiting axis for cross-lab correspondence in this dataset.

## 5.4 Per-Lab Coverage and Confidence Tiers

How many unique compounds does each lab annotate, and how often do
labs converge on the same consensus peak when they do annotate the
same `(compound, adduct)`? The second question requires joining the
curated annotations to the consensus peak table from §3
(`ct_all_samples.csv`).

```{r p5-lab-coverage}
comp_per_lab <- aggregate(compound ~ lab, data = long,
                          FUN = function(v) length(unique(v)))
colnames(comp_per_lab)[2] <- "n_compounds"
knitr::kable(comp_per_lab, caption = "Unique compounds annotated per lab.")
```

```{r p5-lab-coverage-plot, fig.width=5, fig.height=3.5}
#| fig-cap: "**Figure 5.4**: Per-lab annotation count. Differences reflect both detection sensitivity and curation depth."
ggplot(comp_per_lab, aes(x = lab, y = n_compounds)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  geom_text(aes(label = n_compounds), vjust = -0.3) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Unique compounds",
       title = "Compounds annotated per lab") +
  theme_bw()
```

```{r p5-load-consensus}
ct_path <- file.path(base_path, "object", "ct_all_samples.csv")
ct_wide <- read.csv(ct_path, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

ct_long <- do.call(rbind, lapply(labs, function(l) {
  col  <- paste0("chrom_peak_id_", l)
  keep <- !is.na(ct_wide[[col]])
  data.frame(
    consensus_id   = ct_wide$consensus_id[keep],
    sample         = ct_wide$sample[keep],
    n_labs_matched = ct_wide$n_labs_matched[keep],
    lab            = l,
    chrom_peak_id  = as.character(ct_wide[[col]][keep]),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}))

ann_ct    <- merge(long, ct_long, by = c("lab", "chrom_peak_id"), all.x = TRUE)
n_matched <- sum(!is.na(ann_ct$consensus_id))
cat(sprintf("Annotated peaks matched to a consensus group: %d / %d (%.1f%%)\n",
            n_matched, nrow(ann_ct), 100 * n_matched / nrow(ann_ct)))
```

```{r p5-confidence-tiers}
ann_matched <- ann_ct[!is.na(ann_ct$consensus_id), ]

split_stats <- aggregate(consensus_id ~ sample + compound + adduct,
                         data = ann_matched,
                         FUN  = function(v) length(unique(v)))
colnames(split_stats)[4] <- "n_consensus_peaks"

peak_agree <- aggregate(lab ~ consensus_id + compound + adduct +
                              sample + n_labs_matched,
                        data = ann_matched,
                        FUN  = function(v) length(unique(v)))
colnames(peak_agree)[6] <- "n_labs_annotating"

peak_agree_sorted <- peak_agree[
  order(peak_agree$sample, peak_agree$compound, peak_agree$adduct,
        -peak_agree$n_labs_annotating), ]
key <- with(peak_agree_sorted,
            paste(sample, compound, adduct, sep = "|"))
peak_agree_best <- peak_agree_sorted[!duplicated(key), ]

tiered <- merge(split_stats,
                peak_agree_best[, c("sample", "compound", "adduct",
                                    "n_labs_matched", "n_labs_annotating",
                                    "consensus_id")],
                by = c("sample", "compound", "adduct"))

tiered$tier <- with(tiered, ifelse(
  n_consensus_peaks >  1,                              "C: split across peaks",
  ifelse(n_labs_annotating >= 3,                       "A: converged, >=3 labs",
  ifelse(n_labs_annotating == 2,                       "B: converged, 2 labs",
                                                       "D: single lab"))))

knitr::kable(as.data.frame(table(tier = tiered$tier)),
             caption = "Annotation confidence tiers across (sample, compound, adduct) tuples.")
```

```{r p5-tier-plot, fig.width=6, fig.height=3.5}
#| fig-cap: "**Figure 5.5**: Annotation confidence tier distribution. Tier A = ≥3 labs converged on the same consensus peak (strongest cross-lab evidence). Tier C = labs annotated the same (compound, adduct) but disagreed on which consensus peak — co-elution, isomers, or alignment beyond the consensus tolerance."
tier_tab <- as.data.frame(table(tier = tiered$tier))
ggplot(tier_tab, aes(x = tier, y = Freq, fill = tier)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = Freq), vjust = -0.3) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Number of (sample, compound, adduct) tuples",
       title = "Annotation confidence tiers") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))
```

Tier A is the cross-lab ceiling for confident identification. Tier C
indicates places where the cross-platform RT drift exceeds the
consensus-grouping tolerance — the price of the ceiling in identifications.

## 5.5 SIRIUS Structure Rank vs RT Shift

`structure_rank` is the position of the curated standard in SIRIUS's
candidate list (1 = top hit). If residual cross-lab RT variance
matters for downstream identification, peaks with a large RT shift
from the across-lab mean should also show worse structure ranks.

```{r p5-rank-vs-rtshift}
rt_mean <- aggregate(rtmed ~ compound, data = rt_lab,
                     FUN = function(v) mean(v, na.rm = TRUE))
colnames(rt_mean)[2] <- "rt_mean"

rt_shift <- merge(rt_lab, rt_mean, by = "compound")
rt_shift$rt_abs_shift <- abs(rt_shift$rtmed - rt_shift$rt_mean)

rank_rt <- merge(rank_lab, rt_shift[, c("compound", "lab", "rt_abs_shift")],
                 by = c("compound", "lab"))
rank_rt <- rank_rt[!is.na(rank_rt$structure_rank) &
                   !is.na(rank_rt$rt_abs_shift), ]
cat("Rows with both rank and RT shift:", nrow(rank_rt), "\n")
```

```{r p5-rank-vs-rtshift-plot, fig.width=7, fig.height=4.5}
#| fig-cap: "**Figure 5.6**: SIRIUS structure rank (log10) vs absolute RT deviation from cross-lab mean. A trend toward higher ranks at larger RT shifts would indicate that residual RT variance degrades structural identification."
ggplot(rank_rt, aes(x = rt_abs_shift, y = structure_rank, colour = lab)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0, height = 0.15, alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(x = "|RT - cross-lab mean RT| (s)",
       y = "Structure rank (log10)",
       title = "Structure rank vs RT deviation across labs") +
  theme_bw()
```

```{r p5-rank-top-rtshift, fig.width=8, fig.height=4.5}
#| fig-cap: "**Figure 5.7**: The 10 (compound, lab) tuples with the largest RT deviation, with their SIRIUS structure rank. Worst cases are concrete examples of where residual cross-lab variance directly affects identification."
worst_rt <- head(rank_rt[order(-rank_rt$rt_abs_shift), ], 10)
worst_rt$label <- paste0(worst_rt$compound, " (", worst_rt$lab, ")")
worst_rt$label <- factor(worst_rt$label, levels = rev(worst_rt$label))

ggplot(worst_rt, aes(x = label, y = structure_rank)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("Δrt = %.1fs", rt_abs_shift)),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 3) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Structure rank",
       title = "Structure rank for the 10 highest-RT-shift (compound, lab) peaks") +
  theme_bw() +
  expand_limits(y = max(worst_rt$structure_rank) * 1.4)
```

## 5.6 Synthesis

The annotation ceiling has three observable consequences for
multi-centre work:

1. **RT spread per compound** (§5.2) is set by the cross-platform RT
   drift quantified in §3.6 — the same instrumental phenomenon
   surfacing one layer down. Compounds whose spread exceeds the
   consensus-grouping tolerance (15 s) cannot reliably be grouped
   across labs.

2. **m/z spread per (compound, adduct)** (§5.3) is small (mostly
   sub-20-ppm). m/z is not the limiting axis — calibration drift is
   well within tolerance.

3. **Confidence tiers** (§5.4) operationalise the cost: Tier A
   (≥3 labs converging on the same consensus peak) is the headline
   number for high-confidence cross-lab identifications. Tier C (split
   across peaks) is the direct downstream cost of cross-platform RT
   drift exceeding the grouping tolerance.

4. **SIRIUS rank vs RT shift** (§5.5) closes the loop: where the
   residual cross-lab RT variance is high, structural identification
   degrades. This is the *biological* cost of the *technical* ceiling.

Together with Parts 1–4, the picture is consistent across data
levels: intensity variance is large but partly normalisable; RT drift
sets a floor that propagates to the annotation layer; shape variance
is diffuse and non-recoverable; and the residual variance has a
measurable downstream cost in identification confidence.
